subsections.title: Benefits and Features
bullets: PMI reimburses policyholders for medical treatment costs; level of cover depends on what the State provides.
In countries without State healthcare, PMI replaced the fee-for-service model and reduced individuals' financial risk. Cover may or may not be compulsory.
Where a comprehensive State system (e.g. NHS) exists, PMI provides choice over how, where, and when care is delivered — typically for acute/treatable conditions, not chronic or A&E.
PMI can be seen as complementary to (or competing with) State healthcare.

Typical exclusions: drug abuse, self-inflicted injuries, out-patient drugs/dressings, normal pregnancy, cosmetic surgery, gender reassignment, preventative treatment, dialysis, mobility aids, experimental treatments/drugs, organ treatment, war risks, hazardous hobbies.
Exclusions are more common when PMI supplements a State system; harder to enforce where no State system exists (though cosmetic/weight-loss may still be excluded).
Proving an exclusion applies can be difficult (e.g. injury from a listed hobby vs. a domestic accident).
Claims may be paid to the care provider directly, or reimbursed to the insured.
PMI cover varies: where no State healthcare exists, may only cover medical care; where a comprehensive State system exists, may also cover accommodation costs (possibly up to a limit).
PMI is generally annually renewable. Premiums can be rated by age, gender, and prior medical history — subject to local regulation.
Where compulsory, rating factors may be restricted to prevent individuals becoming uninsurable (e.g. Switzerland: no age or medical condition rating permitted).

key_terms.definition: Private Medical Insurance — indemnity-based cover for medical treatment costs.
Pre-PMI model where individuals paid all medical costs out of pocket.
Dangerous hobbies typically excluded from PMI cover.
Policy reviewed and repriced each year.

exam_qa.q: In what ways does PMI compete with a State healthcare system?
exam_qa.a: Competes for the same pool of doctors, nurses, hospital beds, and diagnostic services. Private care uses the same professionals as the State system, so more time spent on private patients reduces availability for State patients.

subsections.title: Insurer's Perspective
bullets: Indemnity basis → risk of very large claims. Imposing policy limits controls this but breaks the true indemnity principle.
Rising claims costs can be managed via HMO/PPO arrangements, though this restricts policyholder choice.
HMO: network of care providers — cover restricted to within the network.
PPO: preferred list of providers — insurer has negotiated preferential rates, reducing costs for both parties.
High/unexpected claim rates can be managed by: excluding pre-existing conditions, underwriting, GP referrals as gatekeepers.
Where underwriting/pre-existing exclusions are prohibited (e.g. compulsory PMI), insurer must ensure the mix of covered lives matches pricing assumptions.
Excesses, coinsurance, and no-claims discounts reduce small claim frequency and drive down premiums.
ACA (USA) example: mandated insurance (individual mandate) to ensure younger/healthier lives are included in the risk pool; also capped annual out-of-pocket expenses.
Wellness incentives (activity tracking, dietary monitoring) can reduce claims — rewards paid as cash, discounts, or premium reductions.
Claim patterns evolve over time (e.g. new diagnostic tests may become expected benefits).
Underwriting is important; level permitted depends on local legislation. Forms must balance comprehensiveness (reducing non-disclosure) against being so onerous they deter sales.

key_terms.definition: Health Maintenance Organisation — care only provided within the network.
Preferred Provider Organisation — insurer-approved list with negotiated rates.
Higher-risk individuals being more likely to take out or retain cover.
US ACA requirement to hold health insurance, with a financial penalty for non-compliance.
Insured retains a fixed percentage of claim costs (e.g. insured pays 20%, insurer 80%).

subsections.title: Meeting Customer Needs
bullets: Where State cover exists, PMI enables faster access, choice of hospital/consultant, and better comfort (e.g. private room).
UK NHS focuses on chronic/life-threatening conditions. Non-emergency procedures (hip ops, cataracts) can involve very long waits — PMI addresses this.
PMI may be perceived as offering cleaner facilities and lower risk of hospital-acquired infections.
6-week waiting list plans: PMI only kicks in if NHS waiting time exceeds 6 weeks — cheaper than comprehensive PMI.
Hospital choice under PMI is often restricted to certain price bands (best choice at highest premiums).
Where State provides no cover, PMI may be compulsory to avoid personal debt or to access treatment at all.
Employers may offer PMI as a benefit to key staff to minimise business disruption from illness.
Products evolve to meet specific customer needs (e.g. waiting list plans as a targeted PMI evolution).

key_terms.definition: PMI that only covers treatment when NHS waiting time exceeds 6 weeks; lower premium than full PMI.

exam_qa.q: Why might someone pay to see a consultant privately if they could see the same consultant via the NHS?
exam_qa.a: To avoid long waiting lists (speed of diagnosis matters clinically and psychologically). May wish to avoid GP referral requirement. More choice of consultant. Private consultants typically allocate more time per patient and may be perceived as offering higher-quality care.

subsections.title: Benefits and Features
bullets: Annually renewable, defined benefit products — primarily UK market.
Benefits paid as cash directly to the policyholder (not as reimbursement to providers).
Covered events: dental, optical, physiotherapy, maternity, hospitalisation, recuperation, hearing aids, consultations.
Cover purchased in 'units' — more units = higher premium.
Waiting periods typically apply before claims can be made (accidents usually excluded from waiting period). Maternity/new child benefit typically requires 12-month wait.
Premiums are community-rated and generally low (fixed benefit product).

key_terms.definition: Fixed cash payment per event — not linked to actual cost incurred.
Same premium charged to all policyholders regardless of individual risk factors.
Period after policy start during which no claims can be made.

subsections.title: Insurer's Perspective
bullets: Fixed benefits reduce risk of very large claims (unlike indemnity products).
More-than-expected claims managed by: waiting periods, pre-existing condition exclusions, no-claims discounts, limits on number of treatments per period.
Where no GP referral required, treatment frequency limits are especially important.
Potential for over-recovery (claim exceeds actual cost) — mitigated by capping benefit (e.g. 50% of medical bill) or applying coinsurance.
Low premiums mean insurer depends heavily on renewals to recover initial acquisition costs and generate profit.
Admin/underwriting costs are significantly lower than PMI.

key_terms.definition: When the defined benefit paid exceeds the actual cost incurred by the policyholder.

subsections.title: Meeting Customer Needs
bullets: Suitable for those who cannot afford full PMI but want cover for specific treatments (dental, optical, physio) without paying full cost.
Not designed for major procedures/operations.
Generally preferred by lower-income individuals.
Complements PMI: PMI typically excludes dental and optical; a cash plan fills this gap.
Fixed cash payment on hospitalisation (private or State) can cover incidental costs such as transport or additional childcare.
Some PMI products now include an overnight hospitalisation cash payment as a standard feature.

subsections.title: Benefits and Features
bullets: USA-specific product (note: UK Glossary defines a different variant).
Comprehensive PMI-type product covering primary, secondary, and tertiary care — but focus is on capping exposure to large costs, not full indemnity.
Introduced in the USA in the 1950s as medical costs rose significantly.
Key innovation: first product to combine hospital, doctor, and ancillary costs under one policy.
Covered services (defined in policy) vary by insurer; in some US States, regulators require a minimum combination of covered services to use the 'major medical insurance' label.
Coinsurance: the insured retains a share of costs — key difference from PMI. This creates a financial incentive to manage care usage.
Example: 10% coinsurance on a $100,000 claim saves the policyholder $90,000.
Deductible (excess): insured pays the first tranche of costs; insurer only pays above this level. Often a percentage of costs above the deductible is also retained by the insured.
Maximum benefit limits may apply (per year, per lifetime, or per benefit category e.g. mental health).
Family policies may apply the deductible at household level.
Comprehensive MME: variant that also covers smaller expenses (closer to full PMI) but may still use coinsurance.
High-deductible MME: protects against rare, very large expenses only.

key_terms.definition: Policyholder retains a fixed percentage of all claim costs (e.g. 10% after deductible).
Amount the policyholder must pay before any insurance benefit is paid (also called excess).
Defined list of treatments/procedures that the policy will pay for.
General term for the portion of claim costs retained by the insured.
MME variant that also covers smaller costs, not just large/catastrophic expenses.

subsections.title: Insurer's Perspective
bullets: Not a true indemnity product — insured always retains a share of costs above the deductible.
However, very large claims are still possible → policy limits and/or reinsurance may be appropriate.
Deductible reduces frequency of small claims reaching the insurer.
Regulation can influence product design (minimum covered services, required coinsurance levels) — partially standardises products, aiding consumer comparison.
Regulatory/political changes create uncertainty: experience takes time to emerge, and consumer behaviour is hard to predict post-change.
Flexibility in product design (varying deductibles, coinsurance levels, benefit types) can attract consumers and improve affordability.
But greater flexibility → more anti-selection (customers self-select coverage they expect to use) and more complex designs are harder to understand and price.
US market issue: some customers buy MME for only a short period, leading to high short-term lapses and reduced profitability.

key_terms.definition: With flexible designs, customers choose the coverage most relevant to them, skewing the risk pool.
Policyholder cancels coverage after a short period, before the insurer has recovered acquisition costs.

subsections.title: Meeting Customer Needs
bullets: Attractive in markets with high medical costs and limited/no State cover or employer-sponsored plans.
Allows small regular premium payments to protect against large unexpected costs.
Especially valuable for families (single family-level deductible).
Limitation: policyholder still faces the deductible (potentially thousands of dollars) and a coinsurance share above it — not full indemnity.
Even comprehensive MME has maximum limits → policyholder remains exposed to very large costs above the cap.
Minimum coverage requirements (where regulated) protect consumers from misleading products and unexpected coverage gaps.
